title: The English Codex
summary: A complete A0 to C2 handwritten English Codex for learners who want to build their own codex, study deeply, review consistently and prepare for Cambridge exams.
html:
Mission

This site is a guide for building your own English Codex by hand. The goal is not to stare at a screen forever, because apparently humanity has suffered enough. The goal is to read each page, copy the essential parts into a personal notebook or binder, create your own examples, review them, and slowly build a serious English reference system from A0 to C2.

The proposal

Everything here is designed to become a handwritten study codex: rules, examples, traps, Cambridge strategies, vocabulary systems, pronunciation notes, review rituals and pop-culture memory hooks. By the end, a dedicated learner should have copied, rewritten, practised and reviewed the whole system by hand.

What is included

AreaWhat it does
A0 → C2 PathAlphabet, numbers, basic grammar, intermediate control, advanced structures, C2 nuance and style.
Handwritten Codex SystemEvery page includes a section designed to be copied by hand into a personal notebook, binder or digital notebook.
Cambridge ForgeFocused on B2 First, C1 Advanced and C2 Proficiency. TOEFL is intentionally outside the scope.
Skill LabsReading, Listening, Speaking, Writing, Vocabulary, Pronunciation and Professional English.
Pop Culture Memory HooksRPG, sci-fi, fantasy, comics, games, films and general nerd culture used as memory anchors, not as copied copyrighted material.

First page to copy

  • Title: The English Codex.
  • Current level: A0 / A1 / A2 / B1 / B2 / C1 / C2.
  • Target exam: B2 First / C1 Advanced / C2 Proficiency.
  • Main rule: I do not only read English. I produce English.
  • Review cycle: 1 day / 7 days / 30 days.
  • Codex principle: The screen teaches. The notebook remembers.

Start today

  1. Choose your current level honestly.
  2. Copy the first page by hand.
  3. Write five personal sentences.
  4. Record one spoken answer.
  5. Add one known mistake to the Error Log.

title: How to Build Your English Codex
summary: The method: read, copy by hand, produce, correct, review and expand your own codex.
html:
The learning loop

  1. Read the page once without copying.
  2. Copy only the Copy by hand section into your English Codex.
  3. Explain the rule in your own words.
  4. Create original examples connected to your life, work, hobbies or studies.
  5. Correct the examples.
  6. Move repeated mistakes to the Error Log.
  7. Review after 1 day, 7 days and 30 days.

Four modes

ModePurpose
Read ModeUnderstand the explanation and examples on the site.
Copy ModeHighlight the notebook-ready parts to copy by hand.
Practice ModeProduce sentences, questions, paragraphs and spoken answers.
Exam ModeConnect the topic to Cambridge tasks.

Memory logic

Treat every grammar topic like a skill tree node. Reading unlocks it. Writing examples levels it up. Correction upgrades it. Review prevents decay, because your brain apparently runs on unstable early-access software.

Copy by hand

  • My English Codex is built by hand, not downloaded into my brain by magic.
  • A page is complete only after I create my own examples.
  • Corrections are XP.
  • Repeated mistakes are data.
  • Review is part of study, not an optional DLC.
  • Speaking and writing must be trained, not admired from a distance.

title: Study Science: Memory, Review and Handwriting
summary: Evidence-informed learning principles adapted for English study.
html:
Core principles

  • Retrieval practice: close the material and recall from memory.
  • Spaced practice: review later instead of cramming everything in one heroic disaster.
  • Interleaving: mix grammar, vocabulary, writing, speaking and listening.
  • Elaboration: connect new information to stories, work, games, scenes and emotions.
  • Generation: create your answer before looking at the model.

Why copy by hand?

Handwriting slows you down enough to select, compress and rephrase. The point is not mystical pen magic. The point is deeper processing. Copying everything mindlessly is just printing with wrist pain. Copying selectively, rewriting, explaining and producing examples is the actual point of the Codex.

Copy by hand

  • Active recall beats passive rereading.
  • Spaced review beats last-minute panic.
  • Personal examples beat copied examples.
  • A notebook becomes useful when it becomes a review system.
  • Every correction must produce a new sentence.

Mini drill

  1. Study one page for 10 minutes.
  2. Close the page.
  3. Write everything you remember.
  4. Open the page and correct your memory.
  5. Write one sentence explaining what you forgot.

title: CEFR Map: A0 to C2
summary: A practical road map of the full learning path.
html:
LevelCodex regionWhat you can do
A0The PrologueRecognise letters, numbers, sounds, basic phrases and sentence skeletons.
A1The ShireIntroduce yourself, describe routines, ask basic questions and survive simple interaction.
A2BreeTalk about past events, future plans, quantity, comparison and everyday needs.
B1RivendellTell stories, explain opinions, connect ideas and handle common work situations.
B2MoriaDiscuss complex topics, argue clearly and write structured texts.
C1Minas TirithUse nuance, advanced grammar, register and professional/academic language.
C2The Undying LandsControl implication, rhetoric, style, precision and sophisticated argument.

Do not skip foundations

Trying to study C1 inversion while A1 questions are bleeding on the floor is not ambition. It is comedy with flashcards.

Copy by hand

  • Draw a road with seven stops: A0, A1, A2, B1, B2, C1, C2.
  • Circle your current level.
  • Write one ability you already have.
  • Write one ability you need next.
  • Choose three pages from your current level.

title: The Error Log
summary: The correction system for repeated mistakes.
html:
The rule

Every repeated mistake must become a logged item: wrong version, correct version, reason, new sentence, review date. Anything less is just hoping, and hope is not a grammar strategy.

WrongCorrectReasonNew sentence
I have 26 years.I am 26 years old.Age uses be in English.I am 26 years old and I work with data.
I have a doubt.I have a question.Doubt does not work like dúvida here.I have a question about this topic.
She work here.She works here.Third person singular takes -s.She works with my team.

Copy by hand

  • Wrong sentence.
  • Correct sentence.
  • Why it was wrong.
  • New personal sentence.
  • Review date.
  • Status: fixed / repeated / dangerous.

title: Notebook Symbols
summary: A visual system for your handwritten notes.
html:
SymbolMeaningUse
✓ RULECore ruleSomething you must apply.
✗ TRAPMistakeA common error or false friend.
PT→ENPortuguese interferenceWhen Portuguese misleads your English.
UPGRADEAdvanced versionA more formal, precise or natural version.
EXAMCambridgeHow this appears in exam tasks.
VOICESpeakingSomething to say aloud.
WRITEWritingSomething to use in texts.
LOOTVocabularyUseful chunk or collocation.

Copy by hand

  • Use the same symbols across the whole notebook.
  • Choose one color for traps.
  • Choose one color for Cambridge notes.
  • Use symbols before long explanations.

title: Alphabet & spelling
summary: Use letters, names, acronyms, emails and spelling in English.
html:
Main idea

Use letters, names, acronyms, emails and spelling in English.

Structure / pattern

A / B / C; dot, dash, underscore, at; Could you spell that?

Natural examples

  • Could you spell your surname?
  • My email is name dot surname at company dot com.
  • IT, HR, CEO, API and KPI are common acronyms.

Brazilian traps

  • Confusing E/I or G/J.
  • Forgetting dot, dash, underscore and at.
  • Using Portuguese letter sounds.

Nerd memory hook

This is the inventory screen before the campaign starts. No alphabet, no quest.

Copy by hand

  • Main idea: Use letters, names, acronyms, emails and spelling in English.
  • Pattern: A / B / C; dot, dash, underscore, at; Could you spell that?

title: Public Site Notes
summary: How to upload and share the project safely.
html:
Before sharing

  • Do not include private personal data in the public version.
  • Avoid official logos, images and long copyrighted quotes from franchises.
  • Use pop culture as metaphor and memory hook, not as copied material.
  • Keep Cambridge information linked to official sources.
  • Test on notebook and desktop screens at 100% zoom.

Upload recommendation

This file is self-contained: HTML, CSS and JavaScript in one file. Upload it as a static page. Rename it to index.html if your hosting platform expects that.

Copy by hand

  • The public site is the guide.
  • The personal notebook is the real Codex.
  • Blank templates become useful only when filled with your own examples.
  • Use the Print Page button when you want a clean reference page.
